next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 9

By 4:47, Eli had already checked out and pulled under the gas-station canopy by the on-ramp. He had been awake before the motel alarm finished its second chirp because he had never really slept. The room’s high-country dry cold still clung to him, along with the heater smell of hot dust and old pennies.

A woman on the local station kept saying words like *advisory* and *deteriorating corridor* while color maps bloomed red over the mountains on the TV above the coffee urn. The map didn’t care about scale. It made the pass look the size of a thumbnail, a quick pinch between one town and the next. He knew better. He had driven enough winters in western Pennsylvania to respect snow, but this wasn’t Pennsylvania snow. This was wind lifting entire shoulders of it and throwing it sideways at steel.

He had pulled on his shirt slowly because the left side of his chest still remembered the awkward hunch from last night, kneeling in sleet to check the front wheel after a Kansas mechanic had done the cheapest brake adjustment he would agree to. The work had bought him less squeal, not confidence. The pedal still felt soft at the end. The mechanic had called it "serviceable if you keep your speed down and your luck up."

Luck was what people named bad math after the fact.

His burner had sat on the sink with one bar left. He had plugged it in while he shaved with hotel soap and looked at himself in the mirror that made everyone look jaundiced. He was fifty-two, with cheeks rough with gray, eyes bloodshot, and a bare ring finger pale where the band had sat for twenty-two y...
open_hooks_to_carry: Inspection scene confirms brake wear he cannot properly afford to fix
Pawn shop exchange strips symbolic protection from the marriage
prev_tail_excerpt:
...nutes later his left tires wandered over the dashed line just as a livestock hauler surged up in the passing lane. The horn detonated beside him. Eli yanked right; the sedan skated on shoulder gravel, rear end sliding half a lane before the tires caught.

He stabbed the brakes to settle it and the pedal sank a soft, spongy inch before grabbing. He dropped twenty miles an hour, hit his hazards, and muscled the car onto the shoulder. His hands shook hard enough to rattle the keys. He pumped the brake pedal three times until it firmed, then sat bent over the wheel and counted ten breaths through his nose before merging back in.

A reflective sign flashed past: `STEEP GRADES WEST OF LIMON - CHECK BRAKES.` This time he read every word. The sign slid into the mirror while he kept both hands welded to the wheel.

A few miles later the steering wheel ticked in his palms, light and rhythmic. He told himself it was just the shoulder seams and kept driving.

The atlas had slid open to Colorado on the passenger seat. Theo's penciled circles glowed pale under the dash light, each one a little target he had promised to hit and did not yet know how to survive.

At 12:37 a.m. he crossed the state line with the tank half full, forty-eight dollars in his wallet, just over eight hundred folded in the envelope under his seat, a legal deadline in his windshield, and a promise already starting to crack. The highway rose almost imperceptibly toward black country he could not see. Behind him Missouri and Kansas flattened into memory. Ahead, the grade steepened.

He did not take the next rest exit.
state_deltas_required: Prepare for mountain travel with failing brakes and too little cash.
He sells his wedding band at a pawn shop for far less than its value and commits to crossing at dawn.
